impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's headache patents claiming methods of treating headache with humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity of Teva's anti-CGRP antibody headache patents, reinstating a jury verdict of willful infringement by Eli Lilly. The case involves blockbuster migraine treatments (Teva's Ajovy and Lilly's competing product) and has major implications for the scope of patent protection for biologic drug classes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent protection for pharmaceutical and biologic innovators by preserving broader genus claims covering classes of therapeutic antibodies, supporting the ability to obtain meaningful patent scope for pioneering drug discoveries.
impacts.negativeImplications: Creates potential exposure for companies developing competing biologics within patented therapeutic classes, as broader genus claims may survive validity challenges more easily.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products had substantial non-infringing uses that would preclude antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under antitrust law by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the damages award, finding the jury properly determined the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Reinforces competitors' ability to challenge anti-competitive patent licensing practices that extend patent monopolies into unpatented product markets, promoting fair competition in complementary product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring licensing agreements that require customers to purchase unpatented products, particularly where those products have uses outside the patent's scope.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions using an emulator and a model of function calls are directed to abstract ideas under Alice step one of the § 101 patent eligibility framework, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict in Columbia's favor, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patents were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly awarded, reinforcing territorial limits on patent damages. The companion case reversed a contempt order against Quinn Emanuel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides defendants with stronger grounds to challenge software and cybersecurity patents under § 101, and limits patent damages exposure for global software companies by reinforcing the territorial scope of U.S. patent rights.
impacts.negativeImplications: University and corporate patent holders face heightened invalidity risk for software-related patents under § 101, and cannot readily recover damages based on worldwide sales of accused products.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and a $2.5 million damages award in favor of GoTV against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities, requiring entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Benefits technology companies facing patent assertions related to adaptive content delivery and streaming by providing a strong precedent for § 101 invalidity of such claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face increased risk that their patents will be found ineligible, even after surviving earlier challenges and obtaining jury verdicts.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether multicore processor patent claims were infringed under the doctrine of equivalents and whether the district court properly granted summary judgment of noninfringement based on extraterritoriality where accused chip design and testing occurred abroad.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's summary judgment of noninfringement of VLSI's multicore processor patent against Intel, finding genuine disputes of material fact on extraterritoriality. The court affirmed the rejection of VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory but reversed the blanket extraterritoriality ruling, remanding for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's rejection of an overly broad extraterritoriality defense, preserving the ability to assert infringement claims where accused products are ultimately used or sold domestically.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies with global semiconductor design and manufacturing operations face continued uncertainty about when foreign activities may give rise to U.S. patent infringement liability.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable pulse oximetry devices, and whether Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order barring importation of Apple Watches with blood oxygen functionality, finding no error in the Commission's infringement findings, validity rulings, or domestic industry determination regarding Masimo's pulse oximetry patents. This upholds a significant import ban on one of Apple's flagship products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent enforcement through ITC proceedings for medical technology innovators, confirming that the domestic industry requirement can be met and that exclusion orders remain a powerful remedy against infringing imports.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies face significant commercial disruption from ITC exclusion orders, particularly when patented features are integrated into high-volume consumer products that cannot easily be redesigned.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify beyond personal knowledge at summary judgment for purposes of the on-sale bar, and whether source code constitutes hearsay under the Federal Rules of Evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment invalidating engine controller reprogramming patents under the pre-AIA § 102(b) on-sale bar, holding that 30(b)(6) deposition testimony is admissible at summary judgment even when the witness lacks personal knowledge, and that computer source code is not hearsay. The court rejected Definitive's argument that the on-sale bar requires the prior art sale to expressly disclose the device's functionality.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in patent cases benefit from expanded evidentiary tools at summary judgment, including 30(b)(6) testimony beyond personal knowledge and admissibility of source code, strengthening invalidity defenses based on prior sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face broader prior art challenges under the on-sale bar, as courts may rely on corporate representative testimony and source code evidence to establish pre-critical-date sales without requiring direct witnesses or explicit functionality disclosures.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in obtaining coiled tubing patents and on Walker Process fraud claims alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of fraudulently obtained patents.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (which had been granted for Global Tubing) and Walker Process antitrust fraud (which had been granted for Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves Tenaris's acquisition of predecessor technology documents and whether it withheld material prior art during patent prosecution.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence on factual development before summary adjudication; defendants in Walker Process claims similarly benefit from the requirement of full factual records.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire predecessor companies' technology face heightened scrutiny regarding disclosure obligations during prosecution, and enforcement of such patents may trigger antitrust exposure through Walker Process claims.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it previously missed the statutory deadline to seek a stay of its original DJ action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay under § 1659(a) by voluntarily dismissing its original declaratory judgment action and refiling a new one. The ruling prevents gamesmanship in parallel ITC and district court patent proceedings involving competing pharmaceutical products for achondroplasia treatment.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from protection against strategic manipulation of parallel proceedings, ensuring that ITC respondents cannot reset statutory stay deadlines through tactical refiling of DJ actions.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day window for a mandatory stay lose the ability to pause district court litigation through refiling, forcing them to litigate on two fronts simultaneously.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether there was a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to support the jury's finding that asserted trade secrets regarding a cosmetic penile implant were protectable trade secrets under California law, and whether the named patent inventors were properly identified.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the jury verdict on trade secret misappropriation, holding that the plaintiff failed to prove its four asserted trade secrets were protectable because the underlying information was generally known in the field. The court also reversed the inventorship correction, finding the purported inventor's contribution was based on generally known ideas. The counterfeiting verdict against Dr. Cornell for unauthorized use of the Penuma® trademark was affirmed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from heightened scrutiny of whether asserted secrets are truly protectable, particularly where the information is arguably generally known in the relevant industry.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on trade secret protection must clearly establish that their claimed secrets are not generally known, or risk losing both trade secret claims and related contractual protections in a single proceeding.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion by excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a damages remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent case against Microsoft involving Azure SmartNIC technology, finding the district court applied an overly rigid standard. The court vacated summary judgment based on absence of a remedy and the denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery, remanding for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more flexible standard for admissibility of damages expert opinions, reducing the risk that technical deficiencies in an expert report will result in case-dispositive exclusion.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending patent cases face continued exposure where damages expert reports that might previously have been excluded under Daubert are now more likely to survive to trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's holding that the PTO Director's instructions governing IPR institution discretion (including the Fintiv framework for parallel litigation) are general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court emphasized the Director's unreviewable statutory discretion over institution decisions and the non-binding nature of the instructions.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued ability to exercise broad discretion in declining IPR petitions without being constrained by formal rulemaking requirements, preserving flexibility in the institution process.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to challenge patents through IPR face continued uncertainty from discretionary denial practices that are not subject to APA rulemaking transparency requirements, potentially limiting access to the IPR system.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the statute. The court held that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of access and that the ITC must evaluate confidentiality designations on a case-by-case basis consistent with statutory requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Increases transparency in trade remedy proceedings, potentially benefiting domestic industries and the public by providing greater access to information about foreign producers' pricing and subsidy practices.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign producers and importers participating in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations face increased risk that commercially sensitive questionnaire responses may be disclosed, potentially chilling candid responses.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute creates a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act, entitling eligible applicants who applied but were not awarded grants to seek damages against the United States.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed denial of the government's motion to dismiss claims by over 300 restaurants that applied for but did not receive Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that the statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in the order applications were received) created an enforceable obligation under the Tucker Act, permitting damage claims against the United States for the SBA's failure to follow the statutory order of priority.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied statutory entitlements due to agency administration errors have a viable path to recover damages, reinforcing government accountability in distributing congressionally mandated relief funds.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potentially significant financial exposure from claims by hundreds of businesses that were statutorily entitled to grants but did not receive them due to administrative failures in processing order.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees to fund expenses unrelated to the PACER system, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund unrelated court technology projects. The court upheld attorneys' fees, costs, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, finding hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides substantial reimbursement to businesses, law firms, and other frequent PACER users who were overcharged, and establishes boundaries on the federal judiciary's fee-setting authority for electronic records access.
